text.title: Benefits Realization Framework
text.summary: The output-outcome-benefit distinction, benefit types, and the six-step benefits realization lifecycle from identification through periodic measurement.

rule.statement: Distinguish outputs (what the project delivers), outcomes (behavioral changes that result), and benefits (measurable improvements); never count a project output as a benefit realized.
rule.rationale: Conflation is the most common failure mode: "CRM system deployed" is an output; "sales team uses CRM for 90% of deals" is an outcome; "20% more deals closed per quarter" is a benefit. Only the last is reportable ROI.

list.item: Output — what the project delivers (e.g., new CRM system).
Outcome — behavioral or operational change enabled by the output (e.g., sales team uses CRM).
Benefit — measurable improvement attributable to the outcome (e.g., 20% more deals closed).
Financial — revenue increase, cost savings, cash flow improvement.
Efficiency — time saved, productivity gains, error reduction.
Quality — fewer defects, higher customer satisfaction, NPS improvement.
Strategic — market position, capability acquisition, optionality.
Compliance — risk reduction, regulatory adherence, audit readiness.

section.title: Six-Step Lifecycle
section.p: Step 1: Identify benefits. During initiation, for each claimed benefit ask: what is the metric, who owns it, and what is the baseline?
Step 2: Quantify. Convert every benefit to a measurable target with a numeric baseline and a named data source. Reject benefits that cannot be quantified.
Step 3: Assign owners. Every benefit needs a named business stakeholder accountable for tracking and realization. The PM facilitates; the owner accounts.

Step 4: Create the realization plan. Define prerequisites (what must be in place), enablers (training, process changes, adoption activities), measurement cadence, and realization curve (expected % realized at M1, M3, M6, Y1, Y2).
Step 5: Track and measure. After go-live, collect actuals on the agreed cadence; compute realization % against the expected curve; apply RAG rubric.
Step 6: Report, adapt, and analyze barriers. When realization deviates, identify root cause: low adoption, training gap, external factor, overpromised target, or attribution error.

rule.statement: Schedule at least five measurement points per benefit: M1, M3, M6, Y1, Y2; a single post-launch snapshot misses benefit decay in year 2 caused by process drift or staff turnover.
rule.rationale: Benefits that look fully realized at month 6 frequently erode by year 2 as early enthusiasts leave, workarounds are reintroduced, or system usage drops without active reinforcement.
